# FinMate — Transaction Categorization Dataset Report (v2)

**Generated by:** `backend/scripts/generate_synthetic_dataset.py`
**Output:** `backend/data/transactions_train_v2.csv`
**Original dataset (unchanged, kept as-is):** `backend/data/transactions_train.csv`

---

## Headline Numbers

| Metric | v1 (current production) | v2 (this report) |
|---|---|---|
| Total samples | 889 | **10,622** |
| Categories | 15 | 15 |
| Real-data samples | 889 (100%) | 889 (**8.4%**) |
| Synthetic-data samples | 0 (0%) | 9,733 (**91.6%**) |
| Max/min class size ratio | ~3.1 (113 / 36) | **1.99** (1,072 / 539) |

## Honest Disclosure on the Real/Synthetic Ratio

The original task target was **70–90% real data, 10–30% synthetic**. That target was **not met** — the actual ratio is the inverse (8.4% real / 91.6% synthetic). This was a deliberate, user-approved decision after a dataset-sourcing audit (see `ML_AUDIT_REPORT.md`, Section 4) found:

- No Kaggle or public dataset exists that labels Indian merchant narrations into a category taxonomy resembling FinMate's 15 categories.
- The closest real alternative (a US credit-card transactions dataset with MCC-style category labels) would require Kaggle API credentials I don't have access to, and even if downloaded, has **zero examples** of India-specific categories (Insurance via LIC, Investment via Zerodha/Groww, UPI transfer phrasing) — meaning it could only have plausibly improved ~7 of the 15 categories.
- Given that constraint, the chosen path was to scale the existing, already-real 889-row hand-curated dataset with **rule-based synthetic generation built from real, publicly known Indian merchant brand names** (not fabricated brand names, not random gibberish) combined with realistic Indian bank-statement narration patterns.

**What "synthetic" means here, precisely:** every synthetic row is `{real brand name} × {realistic narration template}` — e.g. `SWIGGY` × `UPI/{merchant}/{ref}` → `UPI/SWIGGY/483921055`. The brand names are 100% real (Swiggy, Zomato, Uber, LIC, Zerodha, etc.); the *narration formatting* and the *specific instance* (which exact reference number, which exact UPI handle) are generated, not observed in any real transaction log. This is meaningfully different from — and more defensible than — either (a) using the 889 original rows as templates with random noise injected, or (b) generating brand names that don't exist.

**What this means for model evaluation:** test accuracy on this dataset measures the model's ability to recognize *known brand names dressed in varied bank-statement formatting* — a real and useful capability — but does **not** measure generalization to genuinely novel/unseen merchants the way held-out real-world data would. This caveat is carried forward into the Model Benchmark Report (Phase 4) and should be stated in any interview discussion of the resulting accuracy numbers.

---

## Sources

| Source | Sample Count | License | Quality Assessment |
|---|---|---|---|
| `transactions_train.csv` (original FinMate dataset) | 889 | Project-owned (hand-authored for this project) | Real category labels, hand-curated merchant strings mimicking Indian bank narrations. Single-author "voice" — limited formatting diversity per merchant. |
| Synthetic generator (`generate_synthetic_dataset.py`) | 9,733 (post-quality-filter) | N/A — original work, no external data/license involved | Brand names are real and verified against general public knowledge of Indian fintech/retail/transport platforms (Swiggy, Zomato, Uber, IRCTC, LIC, Zerodha, etc. — ~280 distinct real brand names across 15 categories). Narration formatting follows 18 template patterns mimicking UPI/NEFT/IMPS/POS/ACH conventions observed in real Indian bank statements (per the existing `_clean()` preprocessing regex in `ml_categorizer.py`, which already anticipates exactly these prefixes). |

No external/third-party dataset was downloaded or used in this version — `CATEGORY_MAPPING_REPORT.md` was not produced because there was no external category taxonomy to map; this is a deliberate deviation from the original task checklist, documented here rather than fabricated.

## Data Quality Pipeline Results

| Step | Result |
|---|---|
| Input rows (real + synthetic, pre-filter) | 10,850 |
| Null/empty merchant or category removed | 0 |
| Length-outlier removed (<3 alpha chars or >200 chars) | 9 |
| Invalid category label removed (not in canonical 15) | 0 |
| Exact duplicate (merchant+category) removed | 219 |
| **Final row count** | **10,622** |

Merchant string length distribution: min 3, max 51, average 26.2 characters — consistent with realistic Indian bank narration field lengths.

## Merchant Brand Coverage (real brand names used as generation seeds)

~280 distinct real merchant/brand names across 15 categories, including (non-exhaustive):

- **Food (26):** Swiggy, Zomato, Dominos, KFC, McDonald's, Pizza Hut, Subway, Starbucks, Cafe Coffee Day, Burger King, Haldiram's, Barbeque Nation, Wow Momo, Chaayos, Baskin Robbins, FreshMenu...
- **Transport (23):** Uber, Ola, Rapido, IRCTC, Indian Railways, Delhi Metro, BMTC, RedBus, IndiGo, SpiceJet, Air India, Vistara, HP/Indian Oil/BPCL petrol pumps, FASTag, Yulu, Bounce...
- **Shopping (21):** Amazon, Flipkart, Myntra, Ajio, Nykaa, BigBasket, Zepto, Blinkit, Meesho, Croma, Reliance Digital, Decathlon, IKEA, FabIndia...
- **Utilities (16):** Jio, Airtel, Vi, BSNL, BESCOM, Tata Power, Adani Electricity, Mahanagar Gas, Indane, ACT Fibernet, JioFiber...
- **Insurance (13):** LIC (3 variants), HDFC Life, ICICI Prudential, Star Health, Bajaj Allianz, SBI Life, Tata AIA, New India Assurance, Care Health...
- **Investment (14):** Zerodha, Groww, Upstox, Paytm Money, ICICI Direct, HDFC Securities, Angel One, NSDL, CDSL, mutual fund SIPs...
- *(full lists for all 15 categories in `generate_synthetic_dataset.py`'s `MERCHANTS` dict)*

---

## Group-Aware Evaluation Design (added after initial review)

**The problem:** a standard random row-level train/test split would let the same brand — e.g. `SWIGGY` — appear in both train (as `UPI/SWIGGY/846891707`) and test (as `SWIGGY PURCHASE 637500247`). A model can hit very high accuracy under that split just by memorizing brand-name substrings, which inflates the number without proving it can categorize a merchant it has genuinely never seen before — the real production scenario when a user uploads a bank statement with an unfamiliar local merchant.

**The fix:** the CSV now has a third column, `brand` — the literal source brand for every synthetic row, and a best-effort substring-matched brand for every real (v1) row, falling back to a singleton group per unique real merchant string if no known brand matches. Verified correct: all 46 differently-formatted `SWIGGY` rows (`UPI/SWIGGY/...`, `POS SWIGGY PUNE`, `swiggy payment`, etc.) share the single brand key `SWIGGY`.

| | Count |
|---|---|
| Distinct brand groups | 887 |
| Largest brand groups (top 5 sizes) | 101, 99, 95, 93, 93 |
| Singleton groups (mostly real v1 rows with no known-brand substring match) | 683 |

**What this enables (used starting Phase 4):** a `GroupShuffleSplit`/`GroupKFold` evaluation using `brand` as the group key, so entire brands are held out between train and test. This produces two distinct, both-reported metrics:
- **Same-brand accuracy** (standard random split) — measures robustness to narration-formatting variation for *known* brands.
- **Unseen-brand accuracy** (grouped split) — measures genuine generalization to merchants never seen during training. This is expected to be meaningfully lower than the same-brand number, and **is the metric that should be treated as the headline, defensible accuracy** — reporting only the inflated same-brand number would not survive scrutiny.

## Known Limitations of v2

1. **Real-data ratio is 8.4%, not 70-90%** — see disclosure above. This is the single most important caveat for any interview discussion of this dataset.
2. **Single-author template design.** The 18 narration templates were designed by one process (this generation script) — real bank narration conventions vary more than 18 templates can capture, and some banks have idiosyncratic formats not represented at all (e.g., some banks include the payee's registered mobile number, some include a running balance in the same line for certain export formats).
3. **Unseen-brand generalization is now measurable (see above), but the brand pool itself is still small.** Holding out, say, 20% of 887 brands by group still only tests generalization across ~177 held-out brands drawn from the same ~280-brand universe this script knows about — it does not test generalization to a merchant ecosystem the generator has never modeled at all (e.g., a regional sweet shop chain, a local electrician). Grouped evaluation is a real improvement, not a complete solution to the dataset's narrow brand coverage.
4. **English/Latin-script only**, consistent with the original dataset's scope — no regional-language merchant names.

## Recommended Follow-Up (not executed in this pass)

If real Kaggle data sourcing becomes available later (user provides `kaggle.json`), the highest-value addition would be the US credit-card MCC-labeled dataset for the 7 categories that transfer reasonably (Food, Shopping, Transport, Health, Entertainment, Utilities, Other), specifically to get genuinely-unseen-merchant generalization testing for the model. India-specific categories (Insurance, Investment, Rent, Cash, Transfers) will likely always need a synthetic or manually-collected approach since no equivalent public dataset exists.
